elements.sent-2411-002: We present a spectral element model for general-purpose simulation of non-overturning nonlinear water waves using the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations (INSE) with a free surface.
elements.sent-2411-003: The numerical implementation of the spectral element method is inspired by the related work by Engsig-Karup et al. \cite{Allan16 and is based on nodal Lagrange basis functions, mass matrix-based integration and gradient recovery using global $L^2$ projections.}
elements.sent-2411-004: The resulting model leverages the high-order accurate -- possibly exponential -- error convergence and has support for geometric flexibility allowing for computationally efficient simulations of nonlinear wave propagation.
elements.sent-2411-005: An explicit fourth-order accurate Runge-Kutta scheme is employed for the temporal integration, and a mixed-stage numerical discretization is the basis for a pressure-velocity coupling that makes it possible to maintain high-order accuracy in both the temporal and spatial discretizations while preserving mass conservation.
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, the numerical scheme is accelerated by solving the discrete Poisson problem using an iterative solver strategy based on a geometric $p$-multigrid method.
elements.sent-2411-007: This problem constitutes the main computational bottleneck in INSE models.
elements.sent-2411-008: It is shown through numerical experiments, that the model achieves spectral convergence in the velocity fields for highly nonlinear waves, and there is excellent agreement with experimental data for the simulation of the classical benchmark of harmonic wave generation over a submerged bar.
elements.sent-2411-009: The geometric $p$-multigrid solver demonstrates $O(n)$ computational scalability simulations, making it a suitable efficient solver strategy as a candidate for extensions to more complex, real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-010: The ability to accurately model nonlinear water waves is an important aspect in many fields such as oceanography, coastal and offshore engineering, and environmental science.
elements.sent-2411-011: The ability to use numerical simulations of unsteady water waves and flow kinematics is therefore of large interest in these fields.
elements.sent-2411-012: Such simulations can allow for the efficient study of flow characteristics such as wave loads.
elements.sent-2411-013: For simulating unsteady water waves, the most widely used models are based on Navier-Stokes equations (NSE), or on fully nonlinear potential flow (FNPF) models.
elements.sent-2411-014: While FNPF models can be significantly more efficient than full CFD solvers based on NSE \cite{Ransley2019 , they do not take into account viscous and rotational effects and have a tendency to over-predict the wave heights in wave-structure interaction applications due to lack of viscous effects \cite{SriramEtAl2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Hence, in such cases the use of models based on the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations are required and efficient numerical schemes are needed.
elements.sent-2411-016: The full set of NSE can been solved in many ways using different numerical schemes.
elements.sent-2411-017: Some of the conventional methods are based on implicit surface-capturing techniques such as level-set techniques \cite{Osher88,GroossHesthaven06,BIHS2016191 and the volume of fluid (VOF) method \cite{Hirt81 that has been popularized through open-source frameworks such as OpenFoam \cite{WellEtAl1998 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: While these methods have the benefit of being able to handle breaking waves, they are disadvantaged in several ways that affect the computational cost and overall accuracy of the numerical schemes.
elements.sent-2411-019: Despite being widely used, the methods incur high computational costs which limits their use in large scale CFD simulations in both space and time.
elements.sent-2411-020: Moreover, applying pressure boundary conditions to the free surface is inaccurate due to the boundary crossing computational elements \cite{Lin02 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Alternatively, another widely used modeling method is to track the free surface explicitly through a kinematic boundary condition.
elements.sent-2411-022: This way, the free surface elevation is modeled, which opens for designing efficient computational efficient schemes, however, at the price of not being able to handle breaking waves.
elements.sent-2411-023: By explicit tracking the free surface by incorporating a kinematic free surface boundary condition it is possible to keep track of the shape of the spatial domain at all times.
elements.sent-2411-024: Common examples of this is the arbitrary Lagrangian-Eulerian (ALE) method \cite{Donea04 , the mixed Eulerian-Lagrangian method \cite{LonguetHigginsCokelet1976,EngsigKarupMontesering2019 or the $\sigma$-transform method used to transform the vertical coordinate to a regular computational domain, which was first used for CFD wave simulation by Li \& Flemming \cite{Li01 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: It is noted that Decoene \& Gerbeau \cite{Decoene09 showed that the $\sigma$-transform can be seen as a special case of the ALE method.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Li \& Flemming used a 2nd order accurate numerical scheme in both time and space, and coupled pressure and velocity through a projection method, first suggested by Chorin \cite{Chorin68 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Another popular pressure-velocity coupling technique is the Harlow \& Welch algorithm \cite{HarlowWelch1965 that uses a predictor-corrector method to compute velocity and pressure fields, ensuring that the free surface satisfies kinematic and dynamic conditions while maintaining incompressibility.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Recently, higher-order numerical methods have also been used for the $\sigma$-transformed problem, such as Engsig-Karup et al \cite{Allan24 who used a high-order finite difference scheme to solve the free surface Navier-Stokes problem in 2D using a high-order time-stepping method and resolving the pressure through a mixed-stage Poisson problem to achieve a divergence free numerical scheme.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Moreover, Pan et al \cite{Pan21 solved the NSE using a discontinuous Galerkin finite element method (DGFEM), while Melander et al. \cite{Melander24 designed a pseudo-spectral method, both achieving spectral convergence of the error.}}

elements.sent-2411-034: The use of high-order numerical schemes allows for high accuracy and low numerical diffusion at a lower cost for a given accuracy compared to more traditional low-order methods \cite{Kreiss72 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Hence, such schemes are very attractive choices for time-dependent problems where numerical diffusion errors are important to minimize due to build-up of errors in the settings of both large-scale simulations and longer time integration.
elements.sent-2411-036: In relation to these previous works, a motivation behind this work is to apply the SEM to develop a new numerical free surface NSE-based model that can form a basis for wave propagation and wave-structure interaction applications.
elements.sent-2411-037: In CFD solvers, the Poisson problem for pressure often represents a significant computational bottleneck, critically affecting runtime performance.
elements.sent-2411-038: As a result, efficient iterative solvers are essential for effectively resolving the linear systems that arise from the numerical discretization.
elements.sent-2411-039: Iterative multigrid solvers, for instance, are among the most efficient and $\mathcal{O (n)$-scalable algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-040: These solvers capitalize on the ability of traditional stationary iterative methods to efficiently reduce high-frequency errors \cite{Trottenberg01 and are widely used for accelerating advanced CFD solvers \cite{brandt1998barriers,JamesEtAl2002 .}}

elements.sent-2411-045: In this work, we present a novel free surface incompressible Navier-Stokes solver based on the high-order nodal spectral element method.
elements.sent-2411-046: The work can be seen as a multi-domain extension of the single-domain pseudospectral method described in \cite{Melander24 .}

elements.sent-2411-048: Through numerical experiments spectral (faster than algebraic fixed order) convergence is demonstrated together with longer time nonlinear wave propagation over uneven bathymetry.

elements.sent-2411-051: The paper has been organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-052: In Section \ref{sec:goveq we introduce the governing equations for the incompressible Navier-stokes flow with a free surface.}
elements.sent-2411-053: In Section \ref{sec:sigma we introduce the $\sigma$-transform for the coordinates.}
elements.sent-2411-054: In Section \ref{sec:temporal we detail the temporal discretization along with the velocity-pressure coupling achieved by mass conservation through a mixed-stage Poisson problem.}
elements.sent-2411-055: In Section \ref{sec:spatial we detail the spatial discretization using the Spectral Element Method.}
elements.sent-2411-056: The weak formulations of the governing equations are presented along with element and basis construction.
elements.sent-2411-057: In Section \ref{sec:mg we go through the $p$-multigrid method used for the occurring mixed-stage Poisson problem.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Lastly, in Section \ref{sec:res we presents results of the proposed solver both in terms of accuracy of simulations and computational efficiency of the geometric $p$-multigrid method.}

elements.sent-2411-080: To avoid expensive re-meshing and reduce the cost of updating the spatial operations, it is common to employ a $\sigma$-transform which maps the physical domain to a time-invariant reference domain $\Omega^r$.
elements.sent-2411-081: Since the physical spatial $\Omega$ only changes with respect to time in the vertical direction, the vertical mapping is defined as \begin{equation}t^* = t, \quad x^* = x, \quad \sigma = \sigma(t,x,z).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-140: The strong nonlinear terms present in the governing equations can present a challenge for maintaining stability in the simulation, as nonlinear waves can develop unstable sawtooth waves when propagated over time.
elements.sent-2411-141: To avoid this issue, we employ a spectral filtering strategy, taking advantage of the dual nodal-modal representation of the variables.
elements.sent-2411-142: We define a exponential cut-off filter as \begin{equation}S(i) = \begin{cases} 1 0\leq i\leq P_c, \exp\left(\alpha\left(\frac{i-P_c}{P+1-P_c}\right)^\beta\right) P_c<i\leq P. \end{cases}\end{equation} The filter is applied to both the surface elevation $\eta$ and the velocities $u,w$, with the filtered local element solution of any given variable in modal representation defined as \begin{equation}f^n(x)=\sum_{m=1}^{N_{p}}S(i)\hat{f}_m^n\psi(T^n(x)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-143: The filter reduces energy in any modes above the cutoff $P_c$, with parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ determining the strength of the filtering.
elements.sent-2411-144: To maintain the spectral properties of the model, the parameters are generally chosen such that only a few percent of the energy of the highest modes are removed.
elements.sent-2411-145: The modes can easily be obtained through use of the Vandermonde matrices, with the full filtering operation gives as \begin{equation}F = VFV^{-1},\quad F=diag(S).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-149: The Poisson problem for the dynamic pressure has to be solved at every stage of the RK method, and an efficient way to do this is required.
elements.sent-2411-150: The class of multigrid methods \cite{Trottenberg01 takes advantage of the performance of basic stationary iterative solvers, which are great at eliminating high-frequency errors but less so with lower-frequency ones.}
elements.sent-2411-151: By transferring errors to a coarser grid, lower-frequency errors are converted into high-frequency ones, allowing the basic iterative method to be effective once more.
elements.sent-2411-152: Using a high-order spectral method, such as SEM, allows the use of the geometric $p$-multigrid method, which takes advantage of the fact that the coarsening of the grid can be done by simply reducing the polynomial order while keeping the mesh topology unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-153: In this paper, we will restrict the focus to the $V$-cycle, which is illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:vcycle .}
elements.sent-2411-154: Starting at the finest grid, the error of the solution is transferred to coarser and coarser grids, applying smoothing at each of these, until reaching the coarsest grid.
elements.sent-2411-155: Then, the estimated error is transferred back up through the grid levels to the finest grid, again applying smoothing at each level.
elements.figure-2411-002: [H] \includegraphics[width=0.6\linewidth]{vcycle.eps} \caption{Example of $V$-cycle for geometric $p$-multigrid on a $2\times2$ mesh.} \label{fig:vcycle}
elements.sent-2411-156: An integral part of the geometric $p$-multigrid method is establishing the grid hierarchy.
elements.sent-2411-157: As mentioned, this is done by reducing the polynomial order $p$ of the SEM discretization.
elements.sent-2411-158: We let $G^N$ be the original fine grid of order $P_N$ and $G^1$ be the coarsest grid of order $P_1$.
elements.sent-2411-159: The subsequent coarser grids are then generated by reducing the order as \begin{equation}P_{n-1} = ceil\left(\frac{P_{n}+1}{2}\right). \label{eq:gridcoars}\end{equation} For cases where the horizontal and vertical order of discretization differs, a semi-coarsening strategy is employed, reducing the highest order of discretization until both directions have matching orders.
elements.sent-2411-160: A necessity for the geometric $p$-multigrid method is an efficient way to transfer the error between grids.
elements.sent-2411-161: This is done through the use of restriction operators moving the errors from fine to coarse, denoted as $R_{fc}$, and prolongation operators moving the errors from coarse to fine, denoted as $P_{cf}$.
elements.sent-2411-162: In the case of SEM, the modal representation lends itself well to creating linear operators through interpolation.
elements.sent-2411-163: Locally, the solution can be represented on grid $P$ as \begin{equation}f_P^n = V\hat{f}_P^n,\end{equation} where it follows that the solution can be represented on grid $p$ as \begin{equation}f_p^n = \Phi^p\hat{f}_P^n,\end{equation} with $\mathcal{V _{ij = \psi_j(x^P_i)$ and $\Phi^p_{ij = \psi_j(x^p_i)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-164: From these two representations, it follows that any solution can be transferred from grid $P$ to grid $p$ through \begin{equation}f_p^n = \Phi^pV^{-1} f_P^n,\end{equation} meaning we can define the transfer operator as \begin{equation}I_{Pp} = \Phi^pV^{-1}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-165: This means that the prolongation operator is simply defined as \begin{equation}P_{cf} = I_{fc}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-166: While the restriction operator could be defined in the same manner, we instead define it as the transpose of the prolongation operator \cite{Rønquist1987 , \begin{equation}R_{fc} = P_{cf}^T.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-167: The smoothing operator consists of a stationary iterative method which serves to reduce the error at every grid level.
elements.sent-2411-168: An important note is that the iterative method is not applied until convergence, but rather just applied for a given amount of iterations.
elements.sent-2411-169: As mentioned earlier, this allows for fast reductions of high-frequency errors at every grid level.
elements.sent-2411-170: In this work, we apply the additive Schwarz method (ASM) as the smoother.
elements.sent-2411-171: This method has been shown to be an effective smoother for higher order methods such as SEM \cite{AllanLaskowski,Lottes05 .}
elements.sent-2411-172: We can represent the smoothing process as \begin{equation}x^{k+1} = x^k-S^{-1}(Ax^k-b),\end{equation} with $\mathcal{A}$ being the system matrix, and $\mathcal{S ^{-1}$ is the Schwarz preconditioner.}
elements.sent-2411-173: To construct the preconditioner we define transfer operators $\mathcal{R _i: \Omega_k \rightarrow \hat{\Omega _k$ from the original computational domain, to a new subdomain $\hat{\Omega _k$ defined as an element-block combined with a user-determined amount of overlap nodes with neighbouring blocks.}}}
elements.sent-2411-174: This allows us to construct the Schwarz preconditioner as an element-wise block matrix given as \begin{equation}S^{-1}=W\sum_{i=1}^{N_{el}}R_i^T\left(R_iAR_i^T\right)^{-1}R_i.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-175: Here $\mathcal{W}$ is a weight operator defined as \begin{equation}W=\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N_{el}}R_iR_i^T\right)^{-1}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-176: On non-moving meshes, the cost of computing the geometric multigrid operators is usually negligible, as this setup stage is only required at the start of any given simulation.
elements.sent-2411-177: However, due to the movement of our mesh, represented through the $\sigma$-transform, these operators would in principle have to be recomputed at every RK stage.
elements.sent-2411-178: To circumvent this issue, we combine the multigrid solver with both a preconditioned defect-correct method (PDC) \cite{EngsigKarup2014 and GMRES \cite{SS86 , utilizing the multigrid solver to solve the occurring pre-conditioning problem \begin{equation}M^{-1}e = -r.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-179: By basing the pre-conditioning matrix $\mathcal{M}$ on a linearised Navier-Stokes formulation arising from introducing the small--amplitude wave assumption $H/L\ll \mathcal{O (1)$ to construct a time-constant preconditioner, it is only necessary to compute the multigrid operators at the beginning of any given simulation, thus making the computational cost of the setup stage negligible.}
elements.subsection-2411-017: Model verification using nonlinear streamfunction waves
elements.sent-2411-180: In order to verify that the model is capable of achieving the expected spectral $p$-convergence, we here present a convergence study of the error in approximation of the velocity variables.
elements.sent-2411-181: We here assume no viscosity i.e. we let $\nu = 0\text{ [\frac{m^2 {s ]$, which allows us to compare results against analytical stream function solutions \cite{Rienecker81 which are valid solutions for non-viscous and irrotational fluid flow.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-182: We use a fixed number of elements in both the vertical and horizontal direction, and only vary the polynomial order $P$ to demonstrate $p$-convergence.
elements.sent-2411-183: To allow the study to take into account the error from both the Poisson pressure problem, the momentum equations and the free surface equation, the error is computed after one full time step of the RK method, and is defined as \begin{equation}Error = \|u-u_e\|_\infty,\end{equation} with $u_e$ being the exact streamfunction solution.
elements.sent-2411-184: The amount of elements are kept constant at $Ne_x = 20, Ne_y=2$, and the time step size is chosen such that spatial errors dominate.
elements.sent-2411-185: Figure \ref{fig:convvel shows the error convergence for nine different cases.}
